title: Top Five Movie Robots, 'Finch,' and the Awful Truth About Hollywood
format: Brian Raftery joins to talk Tom Hanks and his robot pal Jeff in 'Finch,' then the two draft five favorite movie robots apiece. Plus, Jim Cummings on 'The Beta Test' and what he's learned since 'Thunder Road.'

films.title: Finch
films.note: Tom Hanks as a dying engineer building a robot, voiced by Caleb Landry Jones, to look after his dog. Directed by Miguel Sapochnik. Gentler and more contained than the post-apocalyptic premise suggests. Sean and Brian both single out the robot's parka.

segments.heading: Tom Hanks, Grandfather Now
segments.note: Where Hanks's late-period run fits against his 90s peak

films.title: Cast Away
films.note: The reference point for 'Finch': Hanks acting almost entirely alone.

films.title: Greyhound
films.note: Also premiered on Apple TV+ on a Friday, same pattern as 'Finch.'

films.title: A Beautiful Day in the Neighborhood
The Post
films.note: Brian's pick as a personal favorite, since he grew up in a newspaper family.

films.title: Captain Phillips
films.note: Brian calls it Hanks's best performance, the first time he really let the stoicism crack.

films.title: Road to Perdition
films.note: The closest Hanks has come to a heel turn, and even that was sold softer than intended.

films.title: Magnolia
films.note: Sean's hypothetical: could Hanks have played Frank Mackey? Brian says no.

films.title: Licorice Pizza
films.note: Sean brings up Sean Penn's small, lascivious cameo as the kind of against-type role he'd love to see Hanks try.

segments.heading: Apple TV+'s Movie Problem
segments.note: Good films, no word of mouth

films.title: The Velvet Underground
films.note: Todd Haynes's documentary, the one Apple movie Brian says has actually broken through.

films.title: CODA
films.note: Sean: barely anyone he knows has seen it, despite Oscar buzz.

films.title: Boys State
films.note: Referred to on air as 'Boy State.'

segments.heading: Brian Raftery's Top Five Movie Robots
segments.subject: Movie robots

films.title: Star Wars
films.note: No. 1: R2-D2, 'the most chill character in all of Star Wars.' Compares him to Mike Schmidt on the 1980s Phillies: reliable, surrounded by lunatics.

films.title: Blade Runner
films.note: No. 2: Roy Batty, Rutger Hauer's replicant. The 'tears in rain' speech reads, on rewatch, as the robot telling humans they don't appreciate what they have.

films.title: The Black Hole
films.note: No. 3: Maximilian, Disney's would-be Star Wars cash-in villain robot. Gave Brian nightmares as a kid; still does, apparently, to kids at New Beverly revival screenings.

films.title: Spaceballs
films.note: No. 4: Dot Matrix, voiced by Joan Rivers and performed by Lorene Yarnell. One of Rivers's two best film performances, by Brian's count, alongside Muppets Take Manhattan.

films.title: Westworld
films.note: No. 5: Yul Brynner's gunslinger. Michael Crichton's proto-Terminator, mostly wordless and mostly terrifying.

segments.heading: Sean Fennessey's Top Five Movie Robots
segments.subject: Movie robots

films.title: Star Wars
films.note: No. 1: R2-D2 and C-3PO as a package deal, an Abbott and Costello by way of Kurosawa's 'The Hidden Fortress.'

films.title: WALL·E
films.note: No. 2: Ben Burtt's sound design for the title character, part of Sean's mini tribute to Burtt.

films.title: RoboCop
films.note: No. 3: Paul Verhoeven's satire, plus ED-209, designed by Phil Tippett.

films.title: Ex Machina
films.note: No. 4: Alicia Vikander's Ava, the inversion of Bishop: you spend the movie hoping she's not what you fear she is.

films.title: Aliens
films.note: No. 5: Bishop, played by Lance Henriksen. A sympathetic counterweight to Ash in the first 'Alien,' and later killed off by a different Finch, director David Fincher, in 'Alien 3.'

films.title: Demon Seed
films.note: Julie Christie, directed by Donald Cammell from a Dean Koontz novel: an AI attempting to impregnate a human woman.

films.title: Terminator 2: Judgment Day
films.note: Prompted by a Ringer oral history that sent Brian back to rewatch both films that week.

films.title: The Iron Giant
films.note: Brian didn't appreciate it on release, has come around since.

films.title: Eve of Destruction
films.note: Gregory Hines; Brian calls it the second-weirdest Gregory Hines movie, after Running Scared.

films.title: Deadly Friend
films.note: Wes Craven, starring Kristy Swanson. Home to the basketball death scene that circulated as an early internet gif.

films.title: Vampire in Brooklyn
films.note: Brian's pick for the actual best Wes Craven 'paycheck movie.'

films.title: The Beta Test
films.note: Cummings's new film, about a Hollywood packaging agent unraveling after an anonymous hotel-room encounter, built on testimony from agency staffers he sourced over Twitter.

films.title: Thunder Road
films.note: His breakout, self-financed feature that made French audiences its biggest early buyers.

films.title: The Wolf of Snow Hollow
films.note: His first studio-scale film, with Robert Forster, shot in freezing night exteriors with a 50-to-60-person crew.

films.title: Halloween Kills
films.note: Cummings acts in it: director David Gordon Green cast him after seeing the Thunder Road short on a plane.

films.title: All the Real Girls
films.note: David Gordon Green's film. Cummings says seeing it in high school convinced him a Southern kid could make movies.

films.title: Krisha
films.note: Trey Edward Shults's debut, made for about $35,000 with his own family in the cast, about a Thanksgiving unraveling into addiction. Referred to on air as 'Cretia.'

films.title: Corpus Christi
films.note: The Polish film Cummings names as the last great thing he's seen: a delinquent impersonating a priest.

A Beautiful Day in the Neighborhood.title: A Beautiful Day in the Neighborhood
